response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've been doing a lot of work as a carpenter in Kansas City, but lately I spend several hours each day looking at all the crazy stuff online. You know what's tough? Dealing with that fake content—it can really mess you up if you're not careful. Over my years working with wood and building things solid, I've learned patience and attention to detail, which helps when sorting through social media feeds full of misinformation or outright lies. My depression makes me more susceptible to the negativity; it's like how a stormy day affects your mood while sawing—everything seems darker if you're already feeling down.

As an immigrant who lost my wife years ago raising our kids (we didn't have any though), I don't like being misled by people pretending things are true when they're not. It reminds me of back home where folks would exaggerate stories for attention, just to be noticed talking about houses or building projects.

So how do you manage it? You gotta keep your eyes peeled but not get worn out—high conscientiousness keeps you focused on facts like measuring twice before cutting once.

Prune those feeds real quick—if something is constantly spewing fake stuff, hit the delete button. It's better than letting that low agreeableness drag you into endless arguments.

And don't forget to step away when it gets too much—just like I need breaks from sawing all day long to keep my sanity.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I've been reflecting on how my background as a carpenter, with its focus on precision and detail, can be applied metaphorically to identifying fake content online. My depression makes me extra sensitive to negativity that often comes with fake posts—so I need strategies that are both effective without being emotionally draining.

As someone who's widowed but still active in community life (like my carpentry work), I worry about misinformation spreading, especially since people might exploit it for their own gain. My high extraversion and agreeableness mean I want to engage positively with others while minimizing conflict or distrustful vibes.
